test_cases.purpose: Sample test 1: diamond-shaped graph with two shortest paths of length 3 from 1 to 6.
Sample test 2: simple chain graph. Only one path exists.
Sample test 3: disconnected graph. Node 4 unreachable from node 1.
Graph with a cycle (1-2-3). BFS: dist[1]=0, dist[2]=1, dist[3]=1, dist[4]=2 (via 2), dist[5]=2 (via 3). Shortest path to node 5 is 2 hops (1→3→5).
Single node, no edges. Source equals destination (N=1), so 0 hops needed.
Two nodes, no edges. Node 2 is unreachable from node 1.
Complete graph on 3 nodes (triangle). Direct edge 1-3 gives shortest path of 1.
Self-loop on node 2. BFS ignores the self-loop since node 2 is already visited when processing it. Path: 1→2→3→4 has length 3.
Graph with cycle 1-2-3-1 and cycle 3-4-5-3. BFS: dist[1]=0, dist[2]=1, dist[3]=1, dist[4]=2, dist[5]=2. Shortest path to 5 is 2 (1→3→5).
Breaks DFS wrong approach. DFS (smallest-neighbor-first) finds 1→2→3→5 (length 3), but BFS correctly finds 1→4→5 (length 2).
Graph with cross-edge 2-3 creating multiple paths. BFS: dist[1]=0, dist[2]=1, dist[3]=1, dist[4]=2, dist[5]=2, dist[6]=3. Tests correct level-by-level exploration.
Parallel edges between nodes 1-2 and 2-3. BFS handles parallel edges correctly: dist[1]=0, dist[2]=1, dist[3]=2, dist[5]=3 (via 3→5), dist[4]=3 (via 3→4). Shortest path to 5 is 3 (1→2→3→5).
Minimum non-trivial graph: 2 nodes, 1 edge. Shortest path is 1 hop.
Long chain of 7 nodes. Shortest path from 1 to 7 is 6 hops (the only path).
Chain of 10 nodes. Maximum shortest path in a 10-node connected graph is 9 (Hamiltonian path).
Complete graph K_10 (all 45 edges). Every node is directly connected to every other. Shortest path from 1 to 10 is 1.
Star-like with direct edge 1-10. Despite the chain 1-2-3-...-9, the direct edge gives shortest path 1.
Grid-like 3×3 graph. BFS: dist[1]=0, dist[2]=1, dist[4]=1, dist[3]=2, dist[5]=2, dist[7]=2, dist[6]=3, dist[8]=3, dist[9]=4. Shortest path 1→2→3→6→9 has 4 hops.

stress_test_config.comparison: Run BFS reference solution and brute-force shortest path (repeated BFS or Floyd-Warshall) on each random graph. Compare outputs. Generate random N in [1,50], random M in [0, N*(N-1)/2], random edges with endpoints in [1,N] (may include self-loops and parallel edges).

edge_cases_covered: N=1 single node (source equals destination, answer 0)
N=2 with direct edge (minimum non-trivial, answer 1)
Self-loop on intermediate node (ignored by BFS)
Parallel edges between same pair of nodes
Long chain (maximum shortest path for given N)
wrong_approaches_tested: DFS (first path found): tested by adv_1 — DFS finds path of length 3, correct BFS answer is 2.
Greedy smallest-neighbor: tested by adv_1 — greedy picks 1→2→3→5 (length 3), correct answer is 1→4→5 (length 2).
Dijkstra overkill: not directly tested by a specific case (gives correct answer but is slower). Covered by time complexity analysis.
